sentences.text: --area and south Florida area for 42 years.
Came in 1953.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My husband has been in business, recently retired.
Charlotte Chaney.
Ellner, E-L-L-N-E-R.
No, I did not.
I was born in Perth Amboy, New Jersey, October the 15th, 1921.
My mother is Hungarian.
Her-- her mother and father came over from Budapest and settled in Perth Amboy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother was brought up partly in Perth Amboy and in the Bronx, New York.
That's where she met my father.
She was a cashier in a bakery shop.
My father's family came from Poland.
Yes.
And they were both born here in the States.
I went to a conservative temple in Perth Amboy on Sundays.
It was a small community, and we used to go for Sunday services.
That's right.
We went to Sunday school.
But when it came to Friday night, my mother would bake challahs, I always remember.
She made her own noodles that we had for our chicken soup.
I remember--
Yes.
Her strudel was out of this world.
[CHUCKLES] And I remember her putting it on a-- on a dining room table with a sheet over it and just pulling the dough and making it paper-thin.
And then she would put a sheet over the beds, and when she made her noodles, to dry out the noodles, spread them out.
And coming home from school on a Friday, it always smelled delicious.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
Yes.
I have an older sister that's 22 months older than myself.
No.
No.
No one.
No, we lived in a-- well, when we lived in Perth Amboy, my mother's brother had a leather and finding store.
It was a very large store, which he sold to shoemakers in the surrounding area.
And I remember the leather bins at that time that he had.
They used to buy the leather by the sheets.
And we would put it in the bins.
And we used to play hide and seek.
And-- and we would play in the store.
My mother lived upstairs from the store with my-- with her-- her aunt-- with-- my aunt and uncle had another apartment.
There were two apartments.
Well, in Perth Amboy, the majority of the people there were Hungarian and Polish people.
My mother spoke Polish besides Hungarian and Yiddish.
It was a mixture.
No, it was a mixture.
It was a mixture.
They were a mixture of both.
They were mostly Jewish.
They were, yes.
No.
They were like my mother.
They-- you know, Saturday night, they would have, you know, Saturday night Shabbos dinners.
And it was mostly on the Hungarian side, because my-- my mother's brother married my aunt, who was Hungarian.
She came from New Brunswick, New Jersey.
And I remember on the weekends, we would go to Hungarian picnics that they had.
And I always remember that we would bring big pots of stuffed cabbage--
--We would call toltott kaposzta.
No.
They were mostly the Jewish Hungarians, yes.
No.
None whatsoever.
None whatsoever.
We-- we left Perth Amboy when I was about 13, 14 years old, and we moved to the Bronx.
It was during the Depression.
My father drove a taxi cab.
And we moved into an apartment building.
My mother was taking care of a doctor's office, where she would clean it and keep it.
And on the side, we-- we got an apartment there for her services of taking care of the building.
And I went to junior high school in the Bronx.
And I did take a general course hoping to go on to college.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But things got pretty bad, the Depression.
And the last year of high school, I went to Evander Childs High for a year.
And I went for two years.
And I had an aunt and uncle living down here in Miami.
And my father got a job in-- we went back to Jersey-- with Sears Roebuck in New Brunswick, New Jersey.
And they moved into a furnished room.
At that time, my sister had gone into training in the Beth Israel Hospital in New York.
Into nurses' training.
And I and my cousin-- it was my brother's daughter-- our last year, she graduated Perth Amboy High School.
She was a year older than I was, and I still had another year to go.
And we came down here.
We took a trip down to Miami.
My father was getting resettled.
And as I said, times were very difficult.
And I wrote to my aunt and uncle if we could come down.
And they said, come on down.
And we took a bus.
We traveled down to Miami.
And it was in 1938.
And when we arrived in Miami, in September, the whole city, all you heard was banging and nailing.
And they expected a hurricane.
As we got off the bus, my uncle at that time worked for a small hotel on the beach, the Belmont Hotel, downtown, Miami Beach.
And he hustled us into the hotel.
And all the furniture was up on the second floor.
And my cousin and I, we clung to each other all night, thinking the next day, we'll be blown out into the ocean somewhere.
And that's when this hurricane turned, and it hit up north.
Right.
Well, it was almost toward the end, because we came down near the end of August, beginning of September.
No.
I was not.
No.
Not really.
See, I went to Miami Beach High School.
I finished my last year of high school.
And my cousin had gotten herself a job on Lincoln Road in a baby shop.
And in June, when I graduated, we both came back.
And she decided that she would also go into training, into nurses' training.
It was too expensive to go to college at the time.
I did not take a secretarial course or anything.
I didn't know how to type and-- or take shorthand.
And I felt, well, since she was going in, my sister was in training already, I figured, well, I will follow.
But I had to wait, because I would not have been 18 until October of '39, and you had to be 18 before September.
My cousin went in the-- in the September class, and I went in February of 1940.
About '41 and '42, because so many of the doctors and nurses were leaving to join the army.
Yes, I did.
Yes.
You had to.
Yes, you had to volunteer.
Through the Red Cross.
Yeah.
I mean, they didn't-- we weren't like they do with the soldiers, where they sent you a letter to come.
[CHUCKLES] We went voluntarily.
We wanted to go in.
The service.
That's right.
And they would go in as an officer, a second lieutenant.
And we had to sign up through the Red Cross.
When I graduated, I was-- I went into the operating room.
I was an OR nurse.
During my training, when we went through all the different services, and I went into the operating room, that was my forte.
I just fell in love with surgery, and this is what I wanted to do, become a surgical nurse.
I-- when I joined the army, I went into the Air Corps.
You know, you're young, and you saw all these songs, you know, fly into the sunset.
And I figured, well, I'll join the Air Corps.
And I got orders to go to Montgomery, Alabama for six weeks' training, which I did, where we learned about the army, about marching, about what was expected of us.

And when I finished, they asked us what part of the country-- you know, we did have a choice where we would want to go, And I figured, well-- I said, I'd love to go to Florida, thinking maybe I'd get back down to Miami.
Instead, I was sent to Panama City, Florida, on the west coast, Apalachicola.
It was a gunnery school, and it was a station hospital.
So in October of 1943, that's where I went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the-- this-- to the station hospital.
It was called Tyndall Field.
Well, it really wasn't.
I mean, being in the operating room, it really wasn't, you know, that different.
We-- different operations, maybe.
Well, it was-- it was very good.
It was-- it was very high.
The nurses that I met there, and the officers, and even the GIs, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a blend of everybody, and we all got along very well.
At that time, maybe.
But I didn't hear of any anti-Semitism.
I didn't, you know, realize that there was anything different.
Yes.
Quite a few of the doctors were Jewish, and some of the GIs.
Not that I can recollect.
There may have been conversation, but--
Yes.
I met my husband while I was in training, the last year.
No.
Training in-- at the hospital in Newark, New Jersey.
I went into training at the Beth Israel Hospital in Newark, New Jersey.
And I met my husband in the last year that I was there.
Oh, no.
No.
He-- he had gone into the army in '42.
And at that time, when I went in the army, he was stationed at Camp Bowie, Texas.
He had graduated NYU as an accountant, so he became head of their accounting department.
No, he-- he did try to persuade me-- dissuade me.
Oh, yes.
He wanted to get married.
[CHUCKLES] We should get married.
But you know, what can I tell you?
Young, and the war going on, and I wanted to see the world.
I was going to join the army and join the Air Corps and--
Not-- not at that particular time.
No.
Well, my mother, she was very, very upset.
She said, here she had didn't even have any sons, and here she had to have a daughter that went into the army.
Well, my sister had already-- was already married and had a child.
And her husband was in the navy.
He was stationed in Washington, DC.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it really-- you know, it was just no-- nothing that she had said of--
No.
No.
There was-- she did not-- no, there's no opinion there.
No opinion.
Whatever.
Well, I joined-- I went to the Red Cross in October of 1943 to sign up.
Oh, no, no.
No, no.
We-- I got married in August of 1944.
My husband and I made plans in February of '44.
We both went home.
I got a leave, because officers only had a week's leave.
He was a GI.
He was a T/4.
And he had a two weeks' leave.
So in February, we went home, and we became engaged.
And then when we went back to our respective camps during that time, I sent a letter to my mother, and he sent it to his mother.
And they made the wedding.
They made the plans and got together.
And when I went up in August, everything was set.
And I was married in Newark, New Jersey, August the 20th.
Part-- the family that was there came.
It so happened that my husband had a younger brother who was stationed also in the-- he went into the army.
And it seemed that his mother forgot to send him an invitation, and he didn't get a furlong or a leave, whatever.
So his brother missed our wedding.
But for our 50th anniversary, he was there.
[CHUCKLES]
Yes.
I came back to Florida.
--and he went back to his.
He went back to Texas.
And during that time, I thought, well, most of the general hospitals were stationed in the Midwest, the army hospitals.

So I went to my chief nurse, and I said that I would like to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go into the-- I'll leave the Army Air Corps if I could and go into the-- and go into a general hospital, go into the army.
And she said, well, she would see what she could do about it.
But I figured this way, maybe I would get nearer to Texas or any of the other general hospitals.
That way, we would maybe be able to see each other on a long weekend or whatever.
And when I did get orders, she said, yes, that she could do it, I did not read the small print.
And for 50 years, I read small print, because it said if I did leave the Air Corps, I would be-- I would be ready if they needed me for overseas duty.
And before I even got orders to go to a-- to a general hospital, I had orders to go overseas.
So wonderful, right.
Right.
Well, there was really nothing I could do about it.
The only way that I could get out was to become pregnant.
And it so happened that the unit-- the 127th Evacuation Unit that I was assigned to formed at my husband's camp, which is once in-- one in a million.
They formed the camp at-- the unit at Camp Bowie, Texas.
How they ever got there, I'll never know.

And when I did get there, and they were getting officers and GIs and other nurses from all over the United States to come there to form the unit, we took an apartment in town, where during the week or somewhere, we could be together.
And well, I just didn't get pregnant.
Didn't happen.
[LAUGHS] And I went overseas.
So after, it was like a staging area of a couple months that we all got together, and I just waved goodbye to him with tears in our eyes.
And--
I think it was like anything else, embarking on a new experience of what would be going on.
We did have meetings about it.
We did not know where we were going until the very last minute, because we did get our equipment.
We did get warm clothes, winter clothes.
So we knew we going-- we weren't going to the CBI or the China-Burma area or-- we were headed for the ETO, Theater of Operations, that European Theater.
No.
I was about the same age.
I was 23 at the time.
A few of them were a little bit older, maybe 28.
Some of them were 30.
But--
Yes.
I was.
I was the only Jewish nurse.
No.
Never had any problems, or-- because the majority of the doctors and the GIs came from the New York area.
We had quite a few came from Brooklyn, from the Bronx, Long Island.
And then we had also some doctors coming from the other end, from California and the Midwest.
No, not really.

No, we were-- at that time, I felt-- and even my husband, his father at the time had said, this is-- it's like a Jewish war, and all the Jewish boys should join, should sign up and fight for their rights.
Not very much.
I was not that aware.
That's right.
That's the answer.
I fault our government, very much so.
Well, we were the lead ship going over.
And we went over with the-- I think it was the 17th Armored Division from Texas.
So we had three or four other ships in our convoy.
And when we hit the English Channel, we did not get to England.
We went in-- we landed at Le Havre.
But the night before, or two days before, I remember suddenly they spotted some submarines, and they started to drop the depth charges.
And--
Very scary.
And not only that, but I didn't know how to swim.
[LAUGHS] So I held onto my life belt.
I-- I-- I did not leave it.
I don't know.
But I did not know how to swim at the time.
And-- and I had very poor equilibrium.
Even going today on a ship or on the plane, I always take Dramamine or something.
And the 10 days going over, maybe 8 out of the 10 days I was seasick.
No.
No.
Not that I can remember, no.
I don't know.
I really don't know.
I really don't know.
Well, we finally got into Le Havre.
And they had the landing, the LSTs take us from the ship into Le Havre.
And when we got into Le Havre, they had camps there.
And it was funny.
Why, I don't know, but they were named after cigarettes.
They had Camp Camel, Camp Chesterfield, Camp Lucky Strike at the time.
And our-- our unit, our men stayed in Le Havre at these camps.
And we nurses were put on a bus, rather on a two-ton truck we had.
And we went 50 miles north of Le Havre to a small place called Reims or Neufchatel, that was a little city.
And we got there about 9 o'clock at night.
And it was a chateau.
It was built and shaped in a U form.
It was empty.
And I understand that was used as a school for the children.
They had nuns that took care of them.
But when we got there, it was empty.
Now, where they went, I do not know.
But every-- about every 20 feet, when we got there, there was a potbelly stove.
And the pipe went up, and there was a pane of glass that was out, and out went the-- you know, if we lit them up, the smoke went out the window.
And if the wind blew the other way, the smoke came in.
So half the time, we were filled with smoke or we weren't.
And it was cold.
Yes.
Well, we stayed there.
We were there about 2 months, 2 and 1/2 months.
We got there in January of '45.
We didn't see any of the townsfolk that day.
But the few days following that, very well.
They seemed to accept us, the people that were there, because we-- we still would get up, and we would do calisthenics, and we would march and walk around the town.
And as I said, in the snow and the mud, it was cold.
I don't think I've ever been so cold.
[LAUGHS]
Not very much.
We tried to just amuse ourselves, and, as I said, walk around the countryside.
There really wasn't too much to do.
And we were just sitting there waiting for something to happen.
No.
'45, January of 1945.
We knew that there was fighting going on.
I mean, that's-- no, we didn't know of anything else.
We were just waiting for orders to do something or to go somewhere.
The beginning of March-- the-- around March, our colonel came up to us and said that our unit would be getting ready to leave, that they had gotten orders for us to get together.
And they sent the trucks up, and we put all our equipment in.
We always went with our own footlockers and our bedrolls and our cots went with us.
And--
We started to head south into-- in France.
And I remember in the beginning of April, when we did open up our hospital, we were always out in the field.
We were an evacuation hospital.
